mnemonics.title: Bipartite Check
mnemonics.content: BIPARTITE = No ODD cycles (use 2 colors). Remember: 'BI' means TWO, so only 2 colors needed. If you find an odd cycle, it's NOT bipartite.

mnemonics.title: K(m,n) Edges
mnemonics.content: K(m,n) has m×n edges - just multiply the partition sizes. Remember: K for 'Kount' (count) edges by multiplying!

mnemonics.title: Even Cycles Good, Odd Bad
mnemonics.content: For bipartiteness: Even cycles ✓ (OK), Odd cycles ✗ (Not OK). Think: 'EVEN is GIVEN, ODD is FORBIDDEN'.

mnemonics.title: Star Graph
mnemonics.content: K1,n looks like a star - ONE center connected to n points. Edges = n. Remember: ONE in first set, n in second set.

acronyms.expansion: Complete Bipartite Graph with m vertices in first set and n vertices in second set
acronyms.usage: Used to denote a complete bipartite graph; every vertex in first set connects to every vertex in second set

acronyms.expansion: Star Graph - complete bipartite with 1 vertex in one partition and n in the other
acronyms.usage: Commonly used to represent star topologies in networks
C5 is a 5-cycle; bipartite only when n is even
P4 is a path with 4 vertices; always bipartite regardless of n

visualTips.visualization: Imagine splitting vertices into two groups (Team A and Team B). Draw edges as lines connecting only A→B. Use two colors - all A vertices get Red, all B vertices get Blue. No edge should have same colors on both ends.
Draw m points on left (U) and n points on right (V). Connect EVERY point on left to EVERY point on right with straight lines. You should see a complete connection pattern resembling a rectangle grid.

Draw a triangle (3-cycle) - you cannot 2-color it without conflict (odd). Draw a square (4-cycle) - you can alternate colors Red-Blue-Red-Blue easily (even). The triangle has odd vertices in its cycle.
Draw one dot in center (the '1' in K1,n). Draw n dots in a circle around it. Connect the center to each outer dot. Looks like a star or wheel spokes.

analogies.analogy: Think of a bipartite graph like a dating app with two groups: Group A (males) and Group B (females). An edge exists only if they belong to DIFFERENT groups - like opposite genders at a dance. Same group never connects (no edges within the group).

Finite graphs are like a finite puzzle with limited pieces. Infinite graphs are like the number line - goes on forever. Most practical applications use finite graphs because we work with limited data.
Think of K(m,n) like a round-robin tournament where m teams play all n other teams exactly once. Every team in group A plays every team in group B - complete connectivity!

Bipartite 2-coloring is like painting houses on a street such that no two adjacent houses have the same color. If you can paint with only 2 colors without conflict, the graph (neighborhood relationship) is bipartite.
